era_context: Middle Bronze Age. The Old Babylonian period in Mesopotamia is in its final decades; the Middle Kingdom in Egypt is transitioning from Amenemhat III to Amenemhat IV; the Minoan civilization continues to flourish; the Hittite Old Kingdom is consolidating power in Anatolia. This is a period of relative stability, though the Old Babylonian state is gradually weakening.

events.title: Samsu-iluna's Reign Continues Amid Declining Power
events.description: Samsu-iluna continues to rule Babylon, but the Old Babylonian state is experiencing gradual decline. External pressures from the Kassites and other neighboring powers are increasing. The territorial extent of Babylonian control is shrinking. Administrative records show continued economic activity, but the state's military and political power is waning. This period marks the beginning of the end of the Old Babylonian dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Samsu-iluna's reign is well-attested; the general pattern of decline is clear, but specific year-by-year events are rarely documented.

events.title: Amenemhat IV Becomes Pharaoh of Egypt
events.description: Amenemhat III dies, and Amenemhat IV (r. ~1814–1805 BCE) becomes Pharaoh of Egypt. The transition is smooth, reflecting the institutional stability of the 12th Dynasty. Amenemhat IV continues the building and administrative programs of his predecessor, though with somewhat less vigor. His reign marks the beginning of the gradual decline of the Middle Kingdom, which will accelerate after his death.
events.certainty_note: Amenemhat IV's accession is well-attested; the exact year is uncertain (±5–10 years) but is conventionally placed around 1814 BCE.

events.title: Minoan Trade and Cultural Influence Expand
events.description: The Minoan civilization continues to expand its maritime trade and cultural influence throughout the Eastern Mediterranean. Minoan pottery, artistic styles, and religious practices are adopted or imitated by neighboring cultures. Trade connections with Egypt, the Levant, Anatolia, and the Greek mainland are robust. This period represents the height of Minoan cultural and economic influence.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of Minoan goods and cultural influence in neighboring regions
Egyptian records mentioning trade with Keftiu (Crete)

events.certainty_note: The extent of Minoan trade and cultural influence is well-attested archaeologically; specific year-by-year fluctuations are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the dating of Amenemhat IV's accession remains uncertain (±5–10 years). The interpretation of Minoan cultural influence as primarily peaceful trade has been questioned by some scholars who point to evidence of military conquest or coercive exchange.
historiographic_note: This year falls within a well-documented period for Egypt and Mesopotamia, but year-by-year specificity is limited. The transition from Amenemhat III to Amenemhat IV is well-attested, but the exact year is uncertain. The Minoan civilization is known primarily through archaeology. Chronological uncertainty of ±50–100 years persists for this period.

graph_edges.note: Amenemhat III's death leads to Amenemhat IV's accession.
Minoan trade and cultural influence continue.
